message.content:
The air in Mr. Thorne’s shop was thick with the scent of old paper, brass polish, and dust motes dancing in the single beam of afternoon sunlight. The walls were a chaotic symphony of ticking—grandfather clocks, cuckoo clocks, pocket watches, and wall-mounted mantel pieces, all beating out different rhythms that somehow coalesced into a steady, comforting hum.

Leo, seven years old and wearing sneakers that were slightly too big, stood on his tiptoes to peer over the counter. He held a small, broken toy train in one hand.

"Mr. Thorne?" Leo whispered, though the room was empty except for the old man.

Mr. Thorne didn’t look up. His spectacles were perched precariously on the end of his nose as he bent a tiny gear with tweezers. "Yes, Leo? And please, no shouting. The seconds are trying to rest."

Leo blinked. "Seconds try to rest?"

"They are always working," Mr. Thorne said, finally setting the tweezers down. He picked up a loupe and slid it over his right eye, peering at Leo with a mischievous glint. "Like good soldiers. They march in line, never stopping, never looking back. Why do you ask?"

Leo pointed to the grandfather clock in the corner, its pendulum swinging with a heavy, hypnotic *tock-tock-tock*. "I want time to stop. Just for a minute. So I can finish my drawing. But every time I look up, the sun has moved, and my crayons are dry."

Mr. Thorne chuckled, a sound like dry leaves skittering on pavement. He gestured for Leo to sit on the stool beside him. "Come here. Let me show you something."

He reached under the counter and
